reasoning: Derived from machine afidi (root fidi with a- object prefix) using profession pattern ama- as in amadu dentist from adu tooth; machinist is the tool-user profession.
Root ture from flowers eture (e- plural prefix) and bouquet aturesine (a- collection + ture + sine); singular uses u- prefix as in ushuli principal vs. eshuli principals.
Group of girls uses plural form of girl omukazi, as human plurals denote groups (imukazi girls); unlike non-human collections needing -sine (aturesine bouquet).
Root from cobbler/shoemaker isabamatu (i- craft prefix + saba + ma/tu profession suffixes); shoe is base object root sabatu in instrument class like adu tooth.
Root kamo from kingdom amukamo (a- abstract plural); king is singular human o-/mu- form, parallel to omola child vs. amola children or omuntu man.
imulenzi has imu- plural human prefix matching imukazi girls; boys fits as plural males, unlike fruit (o-/lu- plants) or bridge (u-/a- objects).
aposino has a- prefix for places/abstracts (like ajamuri republic, amukamo kingdom); classroom is an educational place, unlike baggage (i-/a- things) or parent (o-/mu- humans).
iwelemase has i- place prefix matching ishule school; library is a place for books, unlike book (a-/e- object) or librarian (u-/i- agent like ushuli).

answer: Afrhili employs a Bantu-inspired noun class system where prefixes mark singular/plural and semantic categories (e.g., o-/i- for humans, a-/i- for objects/places, e-/u- for certain plurals/singulars). Roots combine productively with these prefixes. Derivations include infixes like ma- for professions (amadu from adu), suffixes like -si for collectives (emelisi fleet), -sine for collections (aturesine bouquet), -ura for leaders (ijamurura), and -li/-tu/-du for agents/instruments (ushuli, isabamatu). Plurals often vowel-shift prefixes; this minimizes unique vocabulary by systematic inflection and derivation.
reasoning: Synthesized from all pairs: prefixes from singular/plural examples (omola/amola, omukazi/imukazi, etc.); suffixes from derivations (emelisi, aturesine, ijamurura, amadu); applied consistently to derive/translate.
